one_line_takeaway: A narrative (non-systematic) survey that catalogs AI/ML/DL/RL and SOAR-based autonomous cloud threat detection and response and their open challenges; useful as an orientation map but contributes no original data, model, or evaluation of its own.
key_finding: The survey's synthesized claim is that AI (supervised/unsupervised/DL/RL/NLP) can shift cloud defense from reactive, signature-and-rule-based, human-driven operation toward autonomous, adaptive, predictive security with higher detection accuracy, fewer false positives, and faster automated response (self-healing, dynamic segmentation, policy-driven mitigation). It concludes the main open problems are data privacy, adversarial ML, model interpretability, integration complexity in heterogeneous multi-cloud, and high initial cost, recommending federated learning and differential privacy, interpretable AI, and human-AI collaboration as future directions. These are qualitative conclusions, not empirically validated by the paper.
core_contribution: A narrative survey (not an empirical study) of AI-driven autonomous threat detection and response for cloud security, published in IJERET 6(4):111-121, 2025 by Sarraf & Pal. Its own contribution is a synthesis/taxonomy that (1) catalogs the cloud threat landscape (data breaches, data loss, malicious insiders, DoS/DDoS, vulnerable APIs, account hijacking, APTs) and the limits of conventional signature-based, rule-based, and manual response; (2) organizes AI detection approaches into supervised ML (decision trees, Naive Bayes), unsupervised ML (clustering, PCA, autoencoders), reinforcement learning (Q-learning, DQN), and deep learning (Bayesian networks, LSTM, neural nets) plus IDS/anomaly detection; (3) describes autonomous response strategies (automated incident handling with SOAR + LLMs like BERT/GPT, self-healing/dynamic network segmentation, policy-driven real-time defense via SIEM/EDR/SOAR); and (4) provides a comparative literature-review table of 8 prior works. It proposes no new algorithm, model, or experiment of its own.
threat_model: The paper frames adversaries against cloud infrastructure exploiting multi-tenancy and distributed architecture: external attackers (DoS/DDoS via botnets, API exploitation, account hijacking, APTs via spear phishing/USB/network penetration), malicious insiders (current/former employees, admins, contractors), and data breaches/loss. It also names threats to the AI defenses themselves: adversarial machine learning (attackers manipulating inputs to evade AI detectors) and data-privacy/regulatory exposure (GDPR, HIPAA) from processing sensitive data. This is discussed narratively, not as a formal threat model with a defined attacker capability set.
limitations: No original evaluation, dataset, or baseline of the authors' own; all quantitative evidence is second-hand from cited papers. The reference list is dominated by low-tier/predatory-adjacent venues and self-citation-style sources, and the text is uneven with grammatical errors and figures described but not analyzed. It offers no systematic search protocol, inclusion criteria, or PRISMA-style methodology, so it is a narrative survey rather than a systematic review. No new taxonomy is formally validated and no reproducible artifact is provided.

quantitative_results: No original quantitative results: the paper is a survey and reports no measurements of its own.
Cited (Torkura et al. 2019, SlingShot) NOT the authors' own: Mean Time to Detect ~30 seconds for both AWS and GCP; Mean Time to Respond ~25 minutes (AWS) and ~90 minutes (GCP).

Cited qualitative claims summarized in Table 1 (e.g., Pitkar 2025, Oduri 2021, Akinloye et al. 2024): AI/ML reportedly improves detection speed/accuracy and reduces false positives and breach likelihood; no numeric values given by the authors.
baselines_compared: None run by the authors. Conceptual comparison only: AI-driven detection/response vs. conventional signature-based detection, rule-based defense, and manual incident response. Table 1 compares eight prior works qualitatively (Pitkar 2025; Akinloye et al. 2024; Mahajan et al. 2023; Sidhu 2023; Ademilua & Areghan 2022; Oduri 2021; Patel et al. 2020; Torkura et al. 2019; Al-Mohannadi et al. 2018).
